sourceMovie.title: Tusk
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Tusk (2014) as a comedy theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The comedy and horror blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Fearless Vampire Killers
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Tusk clicked for you, The Fearless Vampire Killers (1967) is a strong next stop: both trade in comedy and horror, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: John Dies at the End
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Tusk clicked for you, John Dies at the End (2013) is a strong next stop: both trade in comedy and horror, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: 50 First Dates
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Tusk clicked for you, 50 First Dates (2004) is a strong next stop: both trade in comedy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Tusk?
faq.answer: Try The Fearless Vampire Killers, John Dies at the End, 50 First Dates—each shares DNA with Tusk while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Tusk?
faq.answer: Tusk (2014) is best described as Comedy, Horror. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Tusk worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 5.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this comedy lane.
